speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape nine. This is the most happy moment of this interview, because I have on my right my wife, Judith, and behind me, my daughters-- Katherine, my older one, and our baby, the younger one, who is not much younger than Kitty, Vivian-- both married and have children of their own. And that proves a saying which I said at times when we traveled the world and I seen historical spots and locations-- that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means the people are, the people of Israel are alive. And that's exactly what it is-- that after all this terrible things and Holocaust, indeed, the people of Israel, the faith of the Jews goes on. And we build new families and hope that their children and children of them will be good people of this world, God-fearing Jews who will, in somewhat of a modern way nowadays, will understand that our belief was not a crime. But it is a very important step of mankind to go along to develop the civilized world. I also would like to mention here before I will let others talk in my family that the Hungarian life of Jews, from the Middle Ages on, led to somewhat of freedom of achievements, because Jews were slowly accepted into the life of the country. And they were given more rights. They were permitted to live anywhere in cities where they wanted to live. On the other hand, as much as emancipation, and as much as living like every other human being, Jews started to achieve. So did the recognition of that, some of it with-- with admiration, and some of it with hatred and envy. And while all the emancipation and freedom of Jewish life in Hungary reached levels which was hardly seen anywhere in the world or in Europe-- Hungary had a very rich Jewish culture which interwoven with the Hungarian life. On the other hand, in the beginning of this century, the evolvement of laws restricting Jewish freedom and Jewish achievements were indeed terrible things which led to the Holocaust. And not going deep in history, but to try to mention that we are proud of this European Jewish heritage, because that was a very thriving, wonderful culture. And I would end all my interview with holding this little book up, which I found in the concentration camp in the haystack just moments before I was, a day or two before I was freed. And when I came home-- I would like to make alive in my father's words. He just had to put this in because he knew that little prayer book means an awful lot to me. And he wrote, 'My dear son, this prayer book should always lead you with emotions to turn its pages, because God was very good to us and gave us back to your parents, loving Father and Mother.' My wife, Judith my daughters, Kitty, Vivian-- please ask them any questions you like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you want to say about your husband or to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love him. I know it's a-- I know it's a very, very-- must be a very emotional and difficult times for him to do this interview, because he-- not that he didn't talk about it. But we live a very happy life, and we don't dwell on the past. We live with the past. But he felt it was very, very important to do this, not only for him, but for future generations. And it is. It's-- it's important for our children to know, for our grandchildren to know so that it will never again. It will never happen again. As more people find out what happened, I think, because of communication nowadays, it's going to be less like anything like that, the Holocaust, will ever repeat. I was-- I can say, you told me-- I'm also a survivor. I was born in 1942. We were not in camps, but we were hiding with my family in Budapest. Thank god, my mother-- I'm also an only child. My mother, father, grandmother survived. Unfortunately, my grandfather was killed by the liberating Russians the last days of the war. It's a long story. It's another story. But I'm so happy that we are in this country. It gives us freedom of religion. And I'm just thrilled that our daughters are here. And I'm sure they would like to say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to thank the organization that put this together, because we have wanted for a long time to document my father's history. As my mother touched upon, we really grew up with my father making us feel so fortunate for the fact that he survived, my grandparents survived, my great-grandmother survived. So we were never brought up in a house where there were negative vibes or sort of a dark shadow about the war. My father always raised us making it seem as though he didn't want to talk about his story so much, because there were so many stories that were worse than his. And I think that that's why so often he didn't tell it, because he felt so lucky to be alive and that he survived the war with his parents both alive. And it's not something that you'd ever want to sit down with your father and ask him to dredge up, because you don't know the effect that it would have on him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're very grateful that we've been given this opportunity and that our children and their children and children's children will always have this forever-- excuse me-- because I think it was hard to follow my father after the little, the Bible story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, I don't want to speak for her, but I know that we both have great admiration and respect for my father as a man that has nothing to do with him being a Holocaust survivor. It just has a lot more to do with my father as a person, [SOBBING] because-- sorry. We just-- we love you so much. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's mutual.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- sorry. [SOBBING] I just watched a tape that we saw of what people went through in the Holocaust. And it upsets us, because we know my father went through things that he's probably never shared with us. And it-- as a child, it hurts you so much to think that your father saw horrible things that might give him nightmares or upset him or cause him pain when you love someone so much. I'm sorry. I'm getting emotional. If I hadn't seen the tape or [? to ?] this, I would be a little stronger, but I think we've always grown up with this huge sense of being positive Jews, and of always wanting to carry history with us to our children too, so that this would never be repeated and they would never forget. And I know that as a child, we somehow wanted to relate to my father's experiences. I know that I would read so many books. I read all the Chaim Potok books. I read a book called I read a lot maybe because my dad didn't share with us a lot of his experiences, so that we wanted to understand them maybe through other people's experiences. And I think we ended up even teaching our class at school about history because I don't think their parents knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we would share these books with them. I remember getting very upset once in an American history class when they never talked about what happened to the Jews. And the girl next to me had no idea that Jews were even killed during World War II. And this was a nice independent private school here in the area. So it was shocking to me. It felt like we needed to teach them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know that I wouldn't have these strong feelings if it weren't for my father. But I also know, as much as you feel for what my father went through, I must say that, as a child of a Holocaust survivor, it touches your life too, because-- 'guilt' is a very hard word to use, but you feel, in a sense, guilty for not having been through what your parents have gone through their suffering. You grow up in this world where you're given so many materialistic things from your family who want to overdo it for you because they didn't have it as children, that sometimes, as a child, when you want something and you don't get it, or you don't want to practice the piano and you know your father couldn't even have a piano, or you just do the things that every normal child feels-- excuse me. I need a Kleenex. Thank you-- that you have a sense of guilt that you really shouldn't be feeling this way, because your poor parents, or father, didn't have the option of feeling that he didn't have these things that are-- we take for granted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a sense, you'll always have that with you, I think, as well, which is your own little suffering. And it's not something that I mean to tell my father to make him feel bad, because he can't change what history did to him. But it's something that is given to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the end, I want to just conclude by saying that we adore my father and that, in a sense, what happened to him has blessed our lives, because it's made us feel so lucky to be alive. And we know that just our being here too is a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister really said it all. She said everything that is going through my mind. A year ago, when I saw the interview on television, I knew how important it was that my father get his message across as well. And I called. And thankfully a year later, we're all together to be able to have the story told. And it's true. We've never really heard from my father's lips the experiences he went through in quite the detail, I'm sure, that he did today. We've had bits and pieces growing up. And I think it's a testament to my father, in that he wanted to put everything behind him and move on and move forward. And he's created such a strong family and a sense of love and Jewishness and happiness in our family, so that we really don't look back and we don't try and relive a horror in history. But instead, we keep looking forward. But in the same respect, we always have a sense of understanding. And there is an echo constantly that would remind me of what our family went through. And I think that echo is what makes us such strong people and makes my family so special, because we are so small. I don't think we ever take for granted who we are. And for that reason, my father who is a very strong personality and a very loving person and somebody that we always aim to please-- because I think we know how difficult his life was and how much he's giving back to his family and how much he loved his parents. And I think that-- that sense of love and commitment and dedication to family life and Jewishness is-- is what is going to be passed onto our family. And it is very fortunate that we are able to have this story come full circle. And I know it means so much to me to know that my father has finally had his voice and his story told.

speaker: interviewee
text: Isn't there anything else in life which is as important as to hear these voices of my daughters, and eventually, the grandchildren will be growing up just the same way with same morals and same-- same backgrounds of being decent and loving and helpful, as we all try to be? And again, I would like just to close this with saying that I'm a very lucky person. My family is very lucky that we are here. And my parents, who have seen my-- and-- and the other side, Judith's parents, who have seen-- and grandmother, great-grandmother, and now on Judith's parents [? as ?] great-grandparents themselves, who have seen this reemerging Jewish life in our new country-- we can't thank-- more than just saying we can't thank more the opportunity to tell that-- that if we are on the-- on the watch and on-- on the end of teaching and-- and telling about our lives, we done our duty to those who didn't make it and-- and perished in that burning of the Second World War and Holocaust in Jewish history.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's also what I feel, that-- personally, I feel that we have to be the voice of those people who cannot speak anymore, because I think we owe it to them. We owe it to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is showing now is me at age 16. That was just shortly before I was taken to the Jewish house and the time then we had to wear the Jewish stars on our left side of our chest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in 1944, early part of the year, around May, June.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the circumstances of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still lived in, in our house that was before we were moved to the Jewish house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we owned a camera and took pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you get it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how did we get it again? Some of the pictures, I don't know exactly which one, but some of the pictures were sent to relatives in the United States. And when I came to America, eventually, it was given to me by them. So some of the older family pictures are from that time. But this one have survived with some of our belongings, which some Gentile friends were returning to us after the war was over. This picture of me, again, a couple of months later when we were living in the Jewish house. And on the left side on my coat, there is a 10 centimeter Jewish Star of David sewn onto the coat, which was obligatory for us to wear on the streets. And we could not show up on the streets without everybody knowing that there is a Jew going by. And our time to go in the streets was limited from 11 o'clock till 5 o'clock, and later, even restricted further.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the date of the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is probably around beginning of October of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was still found among our belongings which was returned to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On that picture, my parents-- I am about 10 years old at the same time. We went to a town of Miskolc to the wedding of my uncle, Lajos Schleyer. And my parents are very formal as they show up in this tailcoat and top hat in my hand of my father. And I remember the wedding. It was a very happy occasion. And I should say that my uncle didn't survive World War II, whose wedding we went. His wife and children were taken to Auschwitz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of the people in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is my father, Lajos Lichtmann, and my mother, Ida Schleyer before she got married, then Mrs. Ida Lichtmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a probably around '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture-- in the center, my grandmother on my mother's side, and behind her standing, her two daughters. The darker hair, it's my mother in the middle, Ida. And next to her on her left is Margo. And next to my mother on her right is Erwin Schleyer-- he didn't survive World War II, perished. So did Lajos Schleyer, who was a well-known lawyer in the eastern part of the country. And I knew him very well. He was the pride of the Schleyer family. Lajos Schleyer was also a principal of the [INAUDIBLE] district's Jewish school educations. He was a very important lawyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken around 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the circumstances were?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the home on [INAUDIBLE], living happily. This is the picture of graduating-- well, not graduating, but finishing second grade, age 12 at the Budapest Jewish gymnasium with my classmates. And I am myself in the top row on the left hand side with the collars opened on my jacket. And in the center is our teacher, Jeno Grunwald, who was a very respected, strong teacher. And all the classmates, half of them who didn't survive-- and I don't think I will go name by name. But a number of them who survived, I keep in touch with them. Some of them lives in Israel. And two of the best students of the class are on that picture-- Peter Kalman, who lives in Budapest and he's a physician, and [? Ariel ?] [INAUDIBLE], as he has his name now in Israel. His name at the time when this picture is taken is Laszlo Fleischman. And we keep in touch with each other-- wonderful memories, and in memory of those who didn't make it on those picture. This is an invitation which was sent to the United States when I was getting ready, in 1941, for my bar mitzvah. This is the bar mitzvah-- bar-- bar mitzvah invitation. And the text in Hungarian and says that my parents, Lajos and his wife Lichtmann-- Lichtmann-- [? said ?] he invites you to the bar mitzvah of Albert Laszlo, Hebrew name [? Avrohom, ?] on March the 15th. It's an important date in Hungarian history, because it's the same day when one of our freedom fights in Hungary was declared-- very historical day. And it happened to be a Saturday, and in the gymnasium temple, the invitation [? calls ?] [? you ?] [? for. ?] OK. This picture was given to me also in the United States by Julia Schleyer, married to my uncle Gustav Schleyer. And they were in America while we were in Europe during the Holocaust. On the right hand corner is Lojas Schleyer. I mentioned earlier he was a lawyer. And next to it is Gustav Schleyer with Julia. In the left of Gustav Schleyer is the daughter of the lady which is in the lower row on the left hand [? right ?] with the eyeglasses, Aunt Kornel. And they lived in Czechoslovakia. They came to visit the [INAUDIBLE] to see the family. And next to the daughter of Aunt Kornel is her husband, Dr. Grodzsenski, whom I remember when I was about 10, 11 years old visiting [INAUDIBLE]. And then we have also some relatives I cannot identify on the left hand side, standing over Aunt Kornel. And the man on the top [? hat, ?] his name is Samuel Schwartz, who is a-- a gentleman whose relation exactly I do not know. Also, I heard his name many time. Then on his right is my grandmother, Roza Schleyer. And next to her is Juliska Friedmann, married to the gentleman on her right. [? Juliska ?] Friedmann was the one who taught me to read Hebrew at age six before I went to public school. And the gentleman with the bow tie is Dr. Beno Friedmann, who was a wonderful nice old man. I remember him for his good sense of humor and joking with me. He was a physician in the United States. And little I know about, that around 1910, he returned to Hungary giving up his life in America, and married again in Hungary. Both Dr. Friedmann, Beno, and Aunt [? Juliska, ?] the Grodzsenskis all end up-- Lajos Schleyer, all perished during the Holocaust. This is the picture of my grandfather on my mother's side, Albert Schleyer. And this picture is shortly before he died of natural causes. It was around 1913, the picture taken. And I visited his graveyard in the town of [INAUDIBLE] at the Jewish cemetery. And on it, it says that he died in the year of 1913 while World War I was raging on. And he had five sons. All five sons were participating and were enlisted into the war, one of which, [? Sandor-- ?] English name would be Alexander-- died on the battlefield of [? Piave. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my uncle Lajos Schleyer's wedding in the town of Miskolc at the [INAUDIBLE] Hotel, as I can see it on the invitation, which is shown next to it. And on the picture are, starting from the right, this young man is a Zeisler boy, a family member of Klara, who is my-- my uncle's wife on the wedding picture. And next to it, it's me, Albert Lichtmann. Next to it is Agnes Schleyer. Agnes Schleyer is perished-- perished in Auschwitz. We know it for sure. Next to it is Eva Brichta, cousin of mine, alive, lives in the United States. And the other two are also Zeisler children. None of them is alive. And Lajos and his wife Klara and Klara's entire family-- her parents, brothers, family members all perished in Auschwitz. As I mentioned earlier, the countryside, that's where they lived-- Miskolc, [INAUDIBLE]. All those Jews were taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the year of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year of the picture is about 1938. As a matter of fact, if I look at the invitation--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I can see it's 1937. 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Should I close the door?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the wedding picture of Judith and myself. The wedding took place in 1962 in New York. That's perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the wedding of Vivian. And on the right of the picture is Judith and myself. On the left is my in-laws, Judith's parents-- Susan Bardos and Dr. Leslie Bardos. The wedding is taking place in Washington. OK. This is the wedding picture of Katherine, Kitty, with her husband, Evan Dockser. And on Kitty's right is Vivian, the other daughter of ours with her husband, Eric, Eric Keller. And to the left side of Evan is Judith and myself. This is a wedding picture, taking place in Washington, DC, also. This is a little prayer book which I found in the haystack just days before my liberation from the concentration camp. And that's the one I-- I was praying from next morning after I found it. And my prayers were listened to. I was liberated. And I came home. And as the earlier part of this interview tells, I found my father, who was in Budapest. But at the time, we didn't know about Mother. And Father was extremely emotional, obviously, on my return. And I have kept this little prayer book ever since.
